threats.title: Non-ETH _claim Uses Absolute Balance Instead of Swap Delta, Sweeping All Residual ETH
threats.hypothesis_description: In _claim for non-ETH tokens, claimedAmount is set to address(this).balance (absolute balance) rather than the delta from the swap. The _fillQuote function internally computes the correct delta (boughtETHAmount) but never returns it. Any ETH present in the contract from external sources (receive(), selfdestruct, prior refunds) is credited entirely to the current LRT claimer as bonus lpETH. This is a direct code defect in the accounting logic.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code comment 'At this point there should not be any ETH in the contract' acknowledges the assumption. The receive() function accepts ETH from any sender without restriction, directly violating this assumption. Any ETH sent to the contract post-conversion is captured by the next LRT claimer.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _fillQuote internally computes delta (boughtETHAmount = balance_after - balance_before) but doesn't return it; _claim then uses claimedAmount = address(this).balance capturing total balance

preconditions: startClaimDate has been reached (convertAllETH called)
Caller has non-zero balance for a non-ETH allowed token
Contract holds any ETH from receive(), selfdestruct, or other source

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: _fillQuote calculates correct delta at lines 346-351 but discards it; _claim at line 257 uses raw balance instead
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin action required; any LRT claimer can trigger
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent after convertAllETH is called; any ETH arriving at contract is capturable

operational_questions: What realistic sources of post-conversion ETH exist (accidental sends, contract interactions)?
Can an attacker force ETH into the contract via selfdestruct from another contract?
Does the lpETH.deposit function ever refund ETH change?
validation_requirements: Verify _fillQuote does not return boughtETHAmount to _claim
Confirm receive() has no access restrictions
Test claim flow with pre-existing ETH in contract

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Lock minimal LRT tokens during deposit phase
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: After convertAllETH, monitor contract for any ETH (accidental sends, selfdestruct)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call claim(LRT, 100, exchange, data) to capture swap output + all residual ETH
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All residual ETH is minted as lpETH to attacker

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Theft of any ETH accidentally sent to the contract post-conversion
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Unrecoverable - ETH is converted to lpETH for the attacker

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time after ETH appears in contract post-conversion

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ETH balance of contract post-conversion should be monitored
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unusually large lpETH claims relative to LRT token value swapped
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Claims immediately after ETH arrives at contract

threats.title: Zero-Percentage Claim Enables Repeatable Free ETH Capture Without Token Spend
threats.hypothesis_description: The _percentage parameter for non-ETH claims is not validated to be in range [1,100]. With _percentage=0, userClaim = userStake * 0 / 100 = 0, resulting in a zero-token swap. The swap of 0 tokens likely succeeds as a no-op. The user's balance remains unchanged (userStake - 0 = userStake), but claimedAmount = address(this).balance captures any ETH in the contract. This attack is repeatable infinitely since the balance never decreases.
threats.investigation_rationale: _percentage is a uint8 with no bounds check. Combined with address(this).balance capture (OPS-H001), this creates a zero-cost repeatable drain of any ETH that accumulates in the contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 userClaim = userStake * _percentage / 100; no check that _percentage > 0; balance set to userStake - 0 = userStake; claimedAmount = address(this).balance

preconditions: Attacker has any non-zero balance for any allowed LRT token (even 1 wei)
startClaimDate has been reached
Contract holds some ETH (from receive(), selfdestruct, etc.)
0x exchange proxy accepts zero-amount swap calls without reverting

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No lower bound check on _percentage; userClaim rounds to 0 for _percentage=0; balance unchanged
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - any LRT token holder can exploit
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent and repeatable - balance never decreases

operational_questions: Does the 0x exchange proxy accept and succeed on zero-amount swap calls?
What valid _data encoding produces inputTokenAmount=0 that passes _validateData?
Can an attacker force ETH into the contract cheaply to then drain via this exploit?
validation_requirements: Test _validateData with _amount=0 and valid calldata
Test _fillQuote with 0 approval and 0-amount swap via 0x
Confirm balances[user][token] remains unchanged after 0-amount claim

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Lock 1 wei of any allowed LRT token during deposit phase
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: After convertAllETH, wait for ETH to appear in contract
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call claim(LRT, 0, exchange, valid0AmountData) to capture all ETH as lpETH
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat whenever more ETH accumulates - balance is never reduced

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Continuous drain of any ETH sent to contract; infinite repeatable attack
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot be fixed without contract redeployment
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Any ETH accumulation in contract post-conversion

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Trivial - 1 wei of LRT token required
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate - must craft valid 0-amount swap calldata
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time ETH exists in contract post-conversion

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: lpETH minted from claims with no corresponding token swap
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Same address calling claim repeatedly for same token

threats.title: UniswapV3 Calldata ABI Offset Bypass Allows Swap Path Manipulation Past Validation
threats.hypothesis_description: _decodeUniswapV3Data reads the encodedPath bytes from hardcoded calldata offsets, assuming the dynamic bytes parameter offset is always 0x80 (standard). An attacker can craft calldata with a non-standard offset, placing fake path data at the hardcoded position (passing validation) while real path data at the actual offset is used by the 0x exchange proxy. The validator sees fake input/output tokens while the proxy executes with real ones.
threats.investigation_rationale: The assembly code at _decodeUniswapV3Data reads encodedPathLength from a hardcoded position (p+96=offset 132) and derives inputToken/outputToken from it. The 0x proxy follows the actual ABI offset pointer at position 4-35. These diverge with non-standard encoding.

proof_of_signal.pattern: encodedPathLength read from hardcoded offset p+96 (standard=0x80); inputToken from p+128; outputToken from p+encodedPathLength+108. Does NOT read the ABI offset pointer at p-32 to find actual bytes location.

preconditions: Attacker has locked LRT tokens and startClaimDate reached
0x exchange proxy follows standard ABI offset pointers for bytes parameters
sellTokenForEthToUniswapV3 reads path from ABI offset, not hardcoded position

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Assembly reads from hardcoded offsets while ABI standard allows variable offsets for dynamic types
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - any LRT claimer can craft malicious calldata
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent for all non-ETH claims using UniswapV3 exchange

operational_questions: Does the 0x sellTokenForEthToUniswapV3 follow standard ABI offset for the bytes parameter?
Can a real path with different input token exploit residual approvals from prior claims?
What intermediate routing manipulation is possible while maintaining valid input/output tokens?
validation_requirements: Craft calldata with offset != 0x80 and verify validator reads fake path while proxy reads real path
Test whether different input tokens in real path can leverage residual approvals
Verify routing through attacker-controlled pools is feasible

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Craft calldata with ABI offset pointing to alternate position for encodedPath
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Place fake path (valid tokens) at hardcoded position to pass validation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Place real path (manipulated routing) at actual ABI offset position
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Submit claim - validator passes, proxy executes real (manipulated) swap path
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - single on-chain transaction with crafted calldata

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Swap path manipulation; combined with residual approvals could drain other users' tokens
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot detect post-execution without calldata analysis

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Advanced - must understand ABI encoding and 0x proxy internals

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Non-standard ABI encoding in claim calldata
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Swap paths that differ from validated tokens

threats.title: TransformERC20 Lacks Recipient Validation and Transformations Array Is Unchecked
threats.hypothesis_description: When _exchange is TransformERC20, _decodeTransformERC20Data returns only inputToken, outputToken, inputTokenAmount, and selector. The local variable 'recipient' is never assigned (defaults to address(0)), so the recipient check passes trivially. More critically, the transformations array within the calldata is never validated. The entire calldata is forwarded to the 0x exchange proxy, and transformations can include arbitrary sub-operations (e.g., AffiliateFeeTransformer redirecting funds, or PayTakerTransformer).
threats.investigation_rationale: The _validateData function checks recipient != address(this) && recipient != address(0) after the exchange-specific block. For TransformERC20, recipient is never set (remains address(0)), passing the check. The transformations array is part of the forwarded calldata but completely unchecked.

proof_of_signal.pattern: TransformERC20 branch does not assign 'recipient' variable; recipient check at line 310 passes with address(0) default; transformations[] parameter not decoded or validated

preconditions: Attacker has locked LRT tokens and startClaimDate reached
0x exchange proxy has transformers that allow fund redirection (e.g., AffiliateFeeTransformer)
Crafted transformations pass 0x proxy execution requirements

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: TransformERC20 branch at lines 298-305 only checks selector, inputToken, outputToken, inputTokenAmount; no transformations validation

operational_questions: Which 0x transformers are available and can redirect funds to arbitrary addresses?
Can AffiliateFeeTransformer or similar redirect swap output away from the contract?
Can a transformation call back into PrelaunchPoints enabling reentrancy?
validation_requirements: Enumerate available 0x transformers at the deployed exchange proxy
Test crafting transformations that redirect output ETH to attacker address
Test reentrancy via transformation callback to PrelaunchPoints.claim

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Study available 0x transformers at EXCHANGE_PROXY address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Craft TransformERC20 calldata passing validation (correct selector, tokens, amount)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Include transformation that redirects swap output to attacker address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker receives swapped ETH directly; contract receives 0; lpETH.deposit called with 0

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Attacker can redirect swap output away from contract; combined with unchecked transformations could enable reentrancy

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: TransformERC20 claims with unusual transformations array
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Claims where lpETH minted is 0 or near 0 despite large token input
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: External ETH transfers to attacker coinciding with claims

threats.title: Emergency Mode Fails to Enable ETH Withdrawal After convertAllETH - Permanent Fund Lock
threats.hypothesis_description: The withdraw function has an inner check 'if (_token == ETH && block.timestamp >= startClaimDate) revert UseClaimInstead()' that executes regardless of emergencyMode. After convertAllETH sets startClaimDate, ETH depositors can NEVER withdraw ETH via withdraw(), even in emergency mode. They must use claim(), which requires a functional lpETH contract. If lpETH is buggy/malicious, and recoverERC20 blocks lpETH recovery, ETH depositors have zero recovery path.
threats.investigation_rationale: The emergency mode bypass at line 228-232 skips the time window checks but the UseClaimInstead check at line 240 is inside the ETH-specific block, unconditionally executed. This creates a dead-end state where emergencyMode provides no safety net for ETH depositors post-conversion.

proof_of_signal.pattern: emergencyMode bypass at 228-232 skips time checks; but ETH check 'if (block.timestamp >= startClaimDate) revert UseClaimInstead()' at 240 is unconditional within the _token==ETH block

preconditions: convertAllETH has been called (startClaimDate set)
lpETH contract is non-functional (safeTransfer reverts, deposit fails, etc.)
emergencyMode is enabled by owner

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: UseClaimInstead check at line 240 is OUTSIDE the emergencyMode conditional block (lines 228-232)
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Scenario triggered by owner setting buggy/malicious lpETH address
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent after convertAllETH if lpETH is broken

operational_questions: Can recoverERC20 be used to recover lpETH tokens? (No - blocked by NotValidToken check)
Is there ANY recovery path for ETH depositors if lpETH.safeTransfer fails?
Could owner redeploy and migrate, or are funds permanently locked?
validation_requirements: Confirm UseClaimInstead check is outside emergency mode block
Test withdraw(ETH) in emergency mode after startClaimDate is set
Test claim(ETH) with a reverting lpETH contract

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Owner (compromised or malicious) sets lpETH to a contract that accepts deposits but fails on transfers
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: convertAllETH called - all user ETH sent to malicious lpETH
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: ETH depositors cannot withdraw (UseClaimInstead) or claim (lpETH.safeTransfer fails)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Owner enables emergency mode - still blocked by UseClaimInstead for ETH
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires malicious/buggy lpETH address set by owner

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: None - this is a safety mechanism failure
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: ALL ETH depositor funds permanently locked if lpETH is broken
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Impossible without contract upgrade or hard fork - no recovery path exists
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All ETH deposit withdrawals and claims

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: None for triggering (automatic if lpETH is broken)
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Manifests immediately after convertAllETH with broken lpETH

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Emergency mode activation followed by continued failed withdrawals

threats.title: Residual ERC20 Approval to Exchange Proxy Persists After Swap, Enabling Token Theft
threats.hypothesis_description: _fillQuote calls approve(exchangeProxy, _amount) before the swap but never resets the approval to 0 afterwards. If the 0x exchange proxy doesn't consume the full allowance (partial fill, revert-and-catch, or other edge case), the residual approval persists indefinitely. Combined with the UniswapV3 calldata offset bypass (OPS-H003), a subsequent attacker could craft calldata where the validated input token differs from the actual token swapped by the proxy, consuming the residual approval to steal other users' tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _fillQuote function at line 345 sets approval but has no cleanup. Over time, residual approvals for multiple tokens can accumulate. The exchange proxy (0xDef1C0ded9bec7F1a1670819833240f027b25EfF) can spend any token it has approval for.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount)); no subsequent approve(exchangeProxy, 0) after swap completes

preconditions: A prior claim resulted in partial fill or left residual approval
Attacker can identify which tokens have residual approvals (publicly queryable via allowance)
Attacker can craft calldata to exploit residual approval (via OPS-H003 or TransformERC20)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No approval reset after swap; approve overwrites per-token but different tokens accumulate independent approvals

operational_questions: Under what conditions does the 0x proxy leave unconsumed allowance?
Can residual approval be queried on-chain to identify exploitable amounts?
Combined with OPS-H003, can an attacker specify a different input token in the real swap path?
validation_requirements: Test partial fills on the 0x exchange proxy to confirm residual allowance
Verify allowance state after successful swap (full fill vs partial)
Test combined exploit: ABI offset bypass + residual approval

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor IERC20(token).allowance(PrelaunchPoints, exchangeProxy) for all allowed tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: When residual approval detected for token X, craft calldata with offset bypass
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Validator sees attacker's own token Y; proxy executes swap of token X using residual approval
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Token X (belonging to other depositors) is drained; attacker receives ETH/lpETH
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - on-chain monitoring and single transaction

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Other users' deposited LRT tokens stolen via residual approval exploitation

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Advanced - requires ABI encoding and 0x protocol knowledge
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After any partial fill leaves residual approval

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Non-zero allowance(PrelaunchPoints, exchangeProxy) for any allowed token after a claim
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Token balance decrease without corresponding user claim
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Claims by addresses with minimal token balance extracting large value

threats.title: Non-SafeERC20 approve() in _fillQuote Causes Permanent Claim Failure for Non-Standard Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: _fillQuote uses require(_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount)) instead of SafeERC20's safeApprove or forceApprove. Tokens that don't return a bool from approve() (e.g., USDT-style) cause the require to revert because Solidity ABI decoding expects a return value. If such a token is whitelisted via allowToken(), users who locked it can never claim - their tokens are permanently locked since withdraw() is blocked after startClaimDate for non-emergency mode.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contract imports and uses SafeERC20 for transfers (safeTransfer, safeTransferFrom) but switches to raw approve() with require() in _fillQuote. This inconsistency creates a failure path for non-standard ERC20 tokens.
deployment_assumptions: Owner whitelists a token with non-standard approve() return behavior
threats.config_evidence: PrelaunchPoints.sol line 345: require(_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount)) uses raw ERC20.approve instead of SafeERC20; deployment script has empty allowedTokens, tokens added post-deployment via allowToken()

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_sellToken.approve(exchangeProxy, _amount)) - raw approve without SafeERC20, will revert for tokens without bool return

preconditions: Owner allows a token with non-standard approve (no bool return)
Users lock that token during deposit phase
Users attempt to claim after conversion

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: SafeERC20 used everywhere else (lines 8, 170, 250) but raw approve at line 345
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires owner to whitelist non-standard token
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent for affected token - claims always revert

operational_questions: Do any of the target LRT tokens (weETH, ezETH, rsETH, etc.) have non-standard approve?
Can users withdraw via emergency mode as fallback?
Would owner notice and enable emergency mode before significant harm?
validation_requirements: Check approve() return behavior for all intended LRT tokens
Test _fillQuote with a token that doesn't return bool from approve
Verify emergency mode withdrawal works as fallback

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: N/A - this is a code defect, not an attacker-initiated exploit
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Manifests when any user tries to claim tokens with non-standard approve
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All claims for that token revert permanently
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: User tokens locked unless emergency mode is activated

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent claim failure for affected token holders
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Recoverable only via emergency mode withdrawal (returns raw tokens, not lpETH)
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All claims for the affected token type

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverted claim transactions for specific token types
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: No successful claims for a particular allowed token

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token Accounting Mismatch Causes Last Depositor Insolvency
threats.hypothesis_description: When locking non-ETH tokens, _processLock credits balances[receiver][token] += _amount (the requested amount) after safeTransferFrom. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the contract receives _amount minus the transfer fee, but records _amount. Over multiple deposits, the contract's actual token balance becomes less than the sum of all user balances. Late withdrawers or claimers find insufficient balance, causing their transactions to revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: The contract comment says 'ONLY add wrapped LRT tokens. Contract not compatible with rebase tokens' but doesn't mention fee-on-transfer tokens. If owner whitelists one, accounting breaks silently.
deployment_assumptions: Owner whitelists a fee-on-transfer token via allowToken()
threats.config_evidence: PrelaunchPoints.sol line 167: balances[_receiver][_token] += _amount without measuring actual received amount; documentation warns about rebase but not fee-on-transfer

proof_of_signal.pattern: safeTransferFrom(msg.sender, this, _amount) at line 162; balances[_receiver][_token] += _amount at line 167; no balance-before/after delta check

preconditions: Owner allows a fee-on-transfer token
Multiple users deposit that token
Sum of recorded balances exceeds actual contract token balance

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Credits _amount to balance without checking actual received amount after transfer
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires owner to whitelist fee-on-transfer token
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: From first deposit to last withdrawal/claim

operational_questions: Are any target LRT tokens fee-on-transfer?
Could an LRT token be upgraded to add fees after initial whitelisting?
What is the magnitude of accounting mismatch per deposit?
validation_requirements: Test _processLock with a fee-on-transfer token mock
Verify last user withdrawal/claim reverts when balance is insufficient
Check if any planned LRT tokens have fee-on-transfer behavior

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deposit large amounts of fee-on-transfer token to inflate recorded balances
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Withdraw early to claim full recorded balance (contract has enough from all deposits)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Late depositors cannot withdraw - contract balance insufficient for their recorded amounts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: First mover advantage: early withdrawers extract more than their fair share

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Late depositors permanently unable to withdraw/claim full balance
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All deposits, withdrawals, and claims for affected token

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Contract token balance < sum of user balances for that token
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Failed withdrawal transactions for specific tokens

threats.title: convertAllETH Includes Unsolicited ETH and totalLpETH Counts Pre-existing lpETH Donations
threats.hypothesis_description: convertAllETH deposits address(this).balance (which includes unsolicited ETH from receive() or selfdestruct) and sets totalLpETH = lpETH.balanceOf(address(this)) (which includes any pre-existing lpETH sent directly to the contract). Both inflations benefit all ETH depositors proportionally via the mulDiv formula, but distort the 1:1 ETH-to-lpETH conversion ratio. The receive() function unrestricted acceptance of ETH creates a permanent inflation vector.
threats.investigation_rationale: address(this).balance at line 273 may exceed totalSupply if ETH was sent directly. lpETH.balanceOf at line 276 may include donated lpETH. Both inflate the numerator in the claim formula without inflating the denominator.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalBalance = address(this).balance (includes untracked ETH); totalLpETH = lpETH.balanceOf(this) (includes donated lpETH); neither uses delta tracking

preconditions: ETH sent to contract via receive() or selfdestruct before convertAllETH
Or lpETH tokens sent directly to contract before convertAllETH

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: receive() accepts ETH from anyone; convertAllETH uses absolute balances not deltas
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Between contract deployment and convertAllETH call

operational_questions: Is the inflation profitable for any specific actor vs all depositors proportionally?
Can an attacker combine this with withdrawal-and-redeposit during grace period?
What is the maximum distortion an attacker could create?
validation_requirements: Send ETH via receive() before convertAllETH and verify inflated totalLpETH
Send lpETH directly to contract before convertAllETH and verify inflated totalLpETH
Calculate proportional claim impact for all depositors

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Send ETH to contract via receive() before convertAllETH
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: convertAllETH converts totalSupply + extra ETH, generating totalLpETH > totalSupply
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All ETH depositors receive proportionally more lpETH than their deposit
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker benefits only proportionally - not directly profitable unless sole depositor

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot be corrected post-conversion (startClaimDate is irreversible)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Contract ETH balance > totalSupply before conversion
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: ETH sent to contract not through lock functions

threats.title: No Token Disallow Mechanism - Compromised Whitelisted Tokens Cannot Be Removed
threats.hypothesis_description: The allowToken function enables whitelisting tokens but there is no corresponding disallowToken function. Once a token is allowed via isTokenAllowed[token] = true, it cannot be removed. If a whitelisted LRT token is discovered to be malicious, compromised, or upgraded to have harmful behavior (fee-on-transfer, rebase, blacklisting), users can continue depositing it during the deposit phase, potentially corrupting contract accounting.
threats.investigation_rationale: The isTokenAllowed mapping can only be set to true, never back to false. The admin has no mechanism to revoke token whitelist status.

proof_of_signal.pattern: isTokenAllowed[_token] = true; no corresponding function to set isTokenAllowed[_token] = false

preconditions: A whitelisted token becomes compromised or changes behavior
Deposit phase is still active (before loopActivation)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: One-way whitelist; no removal function exists in the contract
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner has no mechanism to fix even if they detect the issue
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During deposit phase for the compromised token

operational_questions: Can any target LRT tokens be upgraded by their protocol to add harmful behavior?
Would the only mitigation be to call setLoopAddresses early to close deposits?
Does emergency mode provide sufficient fallback?
validation_requirements: Verify no disallowToken function exists
Check if setLoopAddresses can be used as emergency deposit halt
Assess upgradability of target LRT tokens

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identified token becomes compromised or adds transfer fee
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Users continue depositing compromised token (no disallow mechanism)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Contract accounting corrupted by non-standard token behavior
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Late withdrawers/claimers cannot recover full balance

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Emergency mode can enable raw token withdrawal
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Deposits and claims for affected token

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Large deposits of a token after behavior change

threats.title: setLoopAddresses Accepts Zero Addresses and Is Irreversible - Potential Permanent Contract Bricking
threats.hypothesis_description: setLoopAddresses has no zero-address validation for _loopAddress or _vaultAddress. It sets loopActivation = block.timestamp, which prevents re-calling (onlyBeforeDate(loopActivation) fails in same or later block). If the owner accidentally sets address(0) for lpETH, convertAllETH will attempt lpETH.deposit{value: totalBalance}(address(this)) on address(0), which will revert. Users can only withdraw during the grace period. If that window passes, funds are locked until emergency mode is enabled.
threats.investigation_rationale: No input validation on critical address parameters in a one-time-call function. The loopActivation = block.timestamp at line 309 prevents correction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: lpETH = ILpETH(_loopAddress); no require(_loopAddress != address(0)); loopActivation = uint32(block.timestamp) prevents re-calling

preconditions: Owner calls setLoopAddresses with address(0) for _loopAddress or _vaultAddress
No mechanism to correct after the call

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No address(0) check; loopActivation reset prevents re-calling; one-shot function

operational_questions: Can emergency mode withdrawal recover all funds if lpETH is address(0)?
Would ETH depositors still be blocked by UseClaimInstead after convertAllETH fails?
Is there any other recovery path?
validation_requirements: Test setLoopAddresses with address(0)
Test convertAllETH with lpETH = address(0)
Verify emergency mode is sufficient fallback

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Owner accidentally (or maliciously) sets lpETH to address(0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: convertAllETH attempts to call address(0).deposit{value:...} - reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Grace period expires; users who didn't withdraw are stuck
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Only recovery: owner enables emergencyMode for non-ETH withdrawals; ETH withdrawals work pre-startClaimDate

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Conversion blocked; deposits locked until emergency withdrawal
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Recoverable via emergency mode (for both ETH and non-ETH before startClaimDate)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: lpETH or lpETHVault set to address(0)
